content:
Flutter

Dart

Mobile Development

Open Source

Programming

5 min read
Just now

--

Detect corners, correct perspective, and filter documents — as data-first APIs, not a fullscreen UI.

If you’ve ever needed to add document scanning to a Flutter app, you’ve probably hit the same wall I did: almost every package on pub.dev is either a fullscreen “black box” that hands you a cropped file path and takes over the whole screen, or a wrapper around Google’s ML Kit that downloads its model through Play Services at runtime — so it isn’t offline, isn’t customizable, and (for the document scanner) is Android-only.
None of them let you own your camera UX and stay offline.
So I built a package that does: document_scan — a composable, native-light, widget-free document scanner. It returns data — a document’s four corners and the processed image bytes — not a screen. You build the camera UI, the overlay, and the capture button exactly how you want.
This article walks through what “widget-free” actually means in practice, how the package is structured, and how you’d use it.

The idea: return geometry, not a screen
Most scanners are UI-first. You launch them, the user scans inside their screen, and you get a file back. That’s great for a drop-in feature — and useless the moment you want a custom flow (a branded camera, a live overlay, a manual-correction step, a review screen of your own).
document_scan inverts that. It's a detection + perspective-correction + filtering pipeline, exposed as plain Dart APIs:

• It never opens a camera. You feed it images or camera frames.

• It returns normalized corners (0..1) and encoded image bytes.

• It has no widgets. Zero. The UI is entirely yours.
That single decision — return data, not a UI — is what makes it composable.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

How it’s built
The package is two independent pieces, plus a thin façade that ties them together:

• DocumentDetector — finds the four corners of a document. It's backed by the platform's own vision engine: Apple Vision (VNDetectRectanglesRequest) on iOS, and an OpenCV contour pipeline on Android. No bundled ML model, no OCR.

• DocumentProcessor — takes those corners and perspective-corrects, crops, and filters the image. This part is pure Dart.

• DocumentScanner — a one-call façade over both, for the common case.
Because the detector and processor are separate, you can use just the part you need — detect corners for a live overlay without ever cropping, or crop with corners a user dragged by hand.
The one-call path
For the simplest case — you have a photo, you want a clean scan:
import 'package:document_scan/document_scan.dart';final scanner = DocumentScanner();final scan = await scanner.scan(ScanInput.file('/path/to/photo.jpg'));
// null when no document-like rectangle is found.
if (scan != null) {
Image.memory(scan.bytes); // scan.bytes is a PNG by default
}One call detects the corners, warps the image upright, applies a “scanned document” filter, and returns encoded bytes. The heavy warp runs on a background isolate automatically, so the UI stays responsive — you don’t reach for Isolate.run yourself.
Live camera stream
The package doesn’t own the camera, so you feed it frames from your own capture (e.g. the camera package). Each frame yields a DetectionEvent — and this is where the design earns its keep:
final subscription = detector
.detectStream(myCameraFrames) // Stream<ScanInput>
.listen((event) {
switch (event) {
case DetectionSuccess(:final corners):
setState(() => _corners = corners); // draw in your overlay
case DetectionEmpty():
setState(() => _corners = null); // "point at a document"
case DetectionSkipped():
break; // a frame was dropped under load — normal, ignore it
case DetectionError(:final error):
debugPrint('detect failed: $error'); // stream stays alive
}
});That sealed DetectionEvent matters. A naive API would return null for "no corners" — but why was it null? No document in frame? A frame dropped because detection was still busy? A native error? Those are three completely different situations for a UI, and collapsing them into null throws away information the consumer needs. The event type keeps them distinct.
Manual correction
Auto-detection isn’t always perfect, so the manual-correction path is first-class. Show the detected corners, let the user drag them, then crop with exactly those — detection is skipped:
final scan = await scanner.scan(input, corners: editedCorners);Detect → adjust → reprocess. That loop is one of the things fullscreen black boxes simply can’t give you.

The part that was harder than the computer vision
Honestly, the OpenCV and Vision work wasn’t the hard part. Turning it into a package others could trust was.
A few decisions that took more thought than the pixels:
Honest platform differences. The two detection engines aren’t identical, and their confidence scores aren’t comparable — Vision gives a real probability, OpenCV has none so it’s a geometric heuristic. Instead of hiding that behind a fake unified number, the package documents it plainly: don’t reuse one confidence threshold across platforms. Hiding the difference would have been easier and more dishonest.
Zero permissions. The package declares no Android permissions and no iOS privacy-manifest APIs. It works on the frames and files you hand it. Any camera permission comes from your capture layer — the package doesn’t drag one in.
Native-light. On iOS it adds nothing to your binary — Apple Vision ships with the OS. On Android it’s just OpenCV’s native library. No ML model to bundle.
Sensitivity and rate control as real APIs. A live camera pushes 30–60 frames a second; running native detection on every one just heats the device. detectStream takes a minInterval to cap the rate, and a DetectionSensitivity (strict / balanced / lenient) that maps to per-platform thresholds.
None of that shows up in a demo GIF. All of it is the difference between “code that works for me” and “a library someone else can depend on.”

Who it’s for
If you’re building a custom scanning flow — KYC and identity capture, receipts, invoices, forms, or any app with its own embedded camera UX — and you want the scan engine without inheriting a fullscreen UI or a Play-Services dependency, this is built for exactly that.
If you just want a drop-in scanner screen and don’t care about owning the UI, a fullscreen plugin is genuinely the faster choice — and that’s a fair trade-off to name.

Try it

• 📦 pub.dev: pub.dev/packages/document_scan

• 💻 Source: github.com/Ozdemiroguz/document_scan
It’s iOS + Android, offline, and device-tested. The repo has a full example app covering gallery scan, a live camera overlay, manual corner editing, reprocessing, and multi-page PDF export.
If you build something with it, I’d genuinely like to hear how it went.
